prompt_md: When designing a program, what should you decide before writing any C++ code?

options.b: The exact inputs, outputs, and sub-tasks the program needs to perform
options.c: How many lines of code the solution will take

explanation_md: The first design step is to clearly state what the program receives (inputs), what it must produce (outputs), and what transformations need to happen in between (sub-tasks). Compiler flags and includes are implementation details that follow naturally once the plan is clear.

`area(4, 5)` returns `20`. `printArea` prints `Area: ` followed by `20` and a newline. The decomposition here follows the design principle: a computation function and a display function each doing one job.

prompt_md: Which approach best matches the bottom-up, incremental testing strategy described in the lesson?

options.a: Write the entire program, then run it once to see if it works
options.b: Write only the most complex function first to get it out of the way
options.c: Start with the smallest sub-task (e.g., just reading input and printing it back), verify it works, then add the next step
options.d: Write `main` last so you know all the helpers compile first

explanation_md: The lesson recommends adding and testing one piece at a time. Starting with the simplest verifiable thing (read input and echo it) means you have a known-good baseline. Each new step is tested before the next is added, so when something breaks you know exactly what changed.

`a + b = 13`, `a - b = 7`. `13 + 7 = 20`. Notice that `(a+b) + (a-b)` always simplifies to `2a`. With `a=10`, the result is `20`. The lesson point: naming the computation in a function makes it easy to test in isolation.

prompt_md: A `main` function that does input reading, three separate computations, and formatted output all in 40 lines — what design principle does that violate?

options.a: Functions should have at most two parameters
options.b: Each distinct step should be a separate function; `main` should read, call, and print
options.d: Output must always be done inside the function that computes the value

explanation_md: The lesson's third mistake covers exactly this: when `main` mixes reading, computing, and printing, the code is hard to follow and hard to fix. The remedy is decomposition — each distinct responsibility becomes its own named function, and `main` becomes a short sequence of readable calls.
